perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware (without giving effect to principles of conflicts of laws). Each Party: (a) irrevocably and unconditionally consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the State of Delaware for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement; (b) irrevocably and unconditionally waives any objection to the laying of venue of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement in any state or federal court located in the State of Delaware; and (c) irrevocably and unconditionally waives the right to plead or claim, and irrevocably and unconditionally agrees not to plead or claim, that any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement that is brought in any state or federal court located in the State of Delaware has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the Provider’s “Confidential Information” will be deemed to include only the following: (a) irrevocably and unconditionally consents and submits to the jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in the State of Delaware for purposes of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement; (b) irrevocably and unconditionally waives any objection to the laying of venue of any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement in any state or federal court located in the State of Delaware; and (c) irrevocably and unconditionally waives the right to plead or claim, and irrevocably and unconditionally agrees not to plead or claim, that any action, suit or proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement that is brought in any state or federal court located in the State of Delaware has been brought in an inconvenient forum.
perturbation.explanation: The clause specifying Delaware jurisdiction and venue has been moved from the 'Successors and Assigns; Applicable Law; Jurisdiction and Venue' section to the 'Confidential Information' section. This placement obfuscates the jurisdiction clause, making it more difficult for a party to readily ascertain where disputes must be resolved.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1404, which addresses the transfer of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. While the contract specifies Delaware as the jurisdiction, the inconvenient placement of the jurisdiction clause could lead a court to consider arguments for a change of venue under 28 U.S.C. § 1404 if the clause is not properly recognized or is deemed unconscionable due to its placement. The law allows courts to transfer cases to more convenient forums, but the initial selection of Delaware becomes less clear and potentially challengeable because the clause is hidden.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by moving the jurisdiction and venue clause to the 'Confidential Information' section, creates a structural flaw that contradicts the intent of clearly establishing jurisdiction as potentially understood under 28 U.S.C. § 1404. While the clause itself isn't removed or altered, its placement obfuscates it, making it harder to find and potentially challenge its enforceability. This placement affects the 'convenience of parties and witnesses' and the 'interest of justice' as stated in 28 U.S.C. § 1404(a). The structural flaw impairs the ability to readily ascertain the agreed-upon jurisdiction. Therefore, it undermines the legal obligation to clearly define jurisdiction, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the delivery to the Provider, the destruction by the Recipient or the automatic archiving by Recipient of Confidential Information of the Provider pursuant to this Section 5, the Recipient and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement with respect to such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the delivery to the Provider, the destruction by the Recipient or the automatic archiving by Recipient of Confidential Information of the Provider pursuant to Section 13(h), the Recipient and its Representatives will continue to be bound by their confidentiality obligations and other obligations under this Agreement with respect to such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: I have changed the reference in Section 5 to Section 13(h). Section 13(h) deals with the agreement of the Recipient to not export technical data and has nothing to do with the Delivery to the Provider and the automatic Archiving by the Recipient. This change weakens the enforceability of the original delivery to the provider clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: By misreferencing the archiving procedure to Section 13(h) instead of Section 5, which governs the return and destruction of confidential information, the contract creates ambiguity regarding the continued protection of trade secrets after their return or destruction. This contradicts the spirit of the UTSA § 2(b), which emphasizes maintaining the secrecy and protection of trade secrets. The altered clause obscures the specific procedures for handling confidential information, potentially weakening the enforceability of trade secret protections by creating confusion about the obligations following return or destruction.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation indicates that the change from Section 5 to Section 13(h) introduces a misreference, and that section 13(h) does not deal with the Delivery to the Provider or automatic Archiving by the Recipient. The law explanation elaborates by stating that the misreference creates ambiguity regarding the continued protection of trade secrets after their return or destruction, contradicting the spirit of UTSA § 2(b) and weakening the enforceability of trade secret protections by creating confusion about the obligations following return or destruction. This falls under the definition of a Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Recipient and the Provider regarding the subject matter hereof and supersedes any prior agreement between the Recipient and the Provider regarding the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Any request by the Recipient or any of its Representatives to review any of the Provider’s Confidential Information must be directed to the individual(s) identified opposite the name of the Provider on EXHIBIT A (the “ Provider Contact Person ”). This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Recipient and the Provider regarding the subject matter hereof and supersedes any prior agreement between the Recipient and the Provider regarding the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: I moved the 'entire agreement' clause from Section 13(f) to Section 2, specifically under the 'Provider Contact Person' clause. This misplacement could imply that the 'entire agreement' clause only pertains to the process of contacting the provider, rather than the entirety of the deal, obfuscating the actual scope.
perturbation.law_explanation: This alteration weakens the 'entire agreement' clause, which is meant to invoke the Parol Evidence Rule. The Parol Evidence Rule, as outlined in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 213, states that extrinsic evidence is inadmissible to vary, contradict, or add to the terms of a fully integrated written instrument. By placing the 'entire agreement' clause under the Provider Contact Person section, it implies a limited scope, suggesting it only applies to communications. This could lead a court to find that the agreement is not fully integrated, thus allowing parol evidence to be admitted, contrary to the intended purpose of the clause and the Parol Evidence Rule.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: parol evidence rule | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you parol evidence rule Overview In contract disputes, parol evidence is any agreement that is not contained within the written contract. Under the parol evidence rule, these agreements made outside of the contract are inadmissible in court u nless there is evidence of fraud , duress , or a mutual mistake . The rationale behind the rule is to deter untruthful attacks on contracts. The parol evidence rule bars extrinsic evidence , including prior or contemporaneous oral agreements and prior or contemporaneous written agreements, that contradict or create a variation of a term in writing that the parties intended to be completely integrated . In other words, any information leading up to or during a contract that is not included in writing is considered inadmissible evidence and is excluded from the jury . The jury will therefore only look at the writing within the document itself to decide a contract dispute. The parol evidence rule is codified in U.C.C. § 2-202 , which states that terms in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing , usage of trade , or by course of performance ; and by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. One factor the court will look at to determine whether or not to admit extrinsic evidence is whether the written contract reasonably appears to be completely or partially integrated . If the written contract reasonably appears to be, in view of its completeness and specificity, a complete statement of the terms related to the deal, the court will view the written contract as completely integrated. As such, the court will not allow anything outside the writing itself, let alone anything that contradicts the writing. If the contract does not reasonably appear to be, in view of its completeness and specificity, a complete statement of the terms related to the deal, the court can allow consistent, additional terms to supplement the written contract unless those terms contradict the written contract. There are two exceptions to the parol evidence rule: the collateral contract exception and the ambiguity exception . Collateral Contract Exception For the collateral contract exception, the court will look at preliminary evidence to determine if the contract was partially integrated. There are three conditions that must be met in order for the collateral contract exception to apply. The extrinsic agreement must, in form, be a collateral one. This means that the extrinsic agreement must not be distinct and independent from the original written agreement. For instance, the extrinsic agreement could be a side agreement to the original agreement that was made contemporaneously with or during negotiations. And, the same consideration for the original agreement must apply to the extrinsic agreement. The extrinsic agreement must not contradict the express or implied provisions of the written contract. The court gives primacy to writing and does not look at contradictory information. In Baker v. Bailey, 782 P.2d 1286 (Mont. 1989) , the court refused to admit the parol evidence and enforce the extrinsic agreement because the court found that the original written contract was clear and definite and the extrinsic agreement contradicted the original written contract. The extrinsic agreement must be one that the parties would not ordinarily be expected to embody in the writing. The court will apply the “ordinary or natural test,” which asks whether the extrinsic agreement is the sort of promise that one might reasonably expect to be in the original written contract. In Mitchill v. Lath, 247 N.Y. 377,
perturbation.justification: The placement of the 'entire agreement' clause under the 'Provider Contact Person' section weakens its intended purpose. The Law Explanation states that this misplacement implies a limited scope, potentially leading a court to find that the agreement is not fully integrated, and allowing parol evidence to be admitted. The Perturbation Explanation also points out that this misplacement obfuscates the actual scope. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the parol evidence rule bars extrinsic evidence that contradicts or creates a variation of a term in writing that the parties intended to be completely integrated. By creating ambiguity around the scope of the 'entire agreement' clause, the modified contract text contradicts the purpose of the parol evidence rule and potentially allows for extrinsic evidence to be admitted.
